title: General Emergency First Response
steps: 1. Make sure the scene is safe for you, the injured person and bystanders before you go closer.
2. Call emergency services early if the person is unconscious, not breathing normally, severely injured or getting worse.
3. Use a simple ABCDE check: open the airway, look for breathing problems, look for major bleeding or shock, check mental status, then expose injuries while preventing heat loss.
4. If the person is unconscious and there is no trauma concern, place them in the recovery position and keep the airway clear.
5. Control any life-threatening external bleeding, keep the person still, reassure them and protect them from getting too hot or too cold.
6. Stay with the person, re-check breathing and responsiveness often, and hand over clearly when professional help arrives.
warning: Do not move the person unless there is immediate danger or you must move them to open the airway or perform lifesaving care.
call_ambulance_if: the person is unconscious, confused or hard to wake
breathing is absent, slow, noisy or difficult
there is major bleeding, severe pain or obvious serious injury
the condition is worsening or you are unsure the person is safe to leave

title: Severe Bleeding
steps: 1. Ensure the area is safe, then expose the wound and look for the source of bleeding.
2. Apply firm direct pressure with a clean cloth, dressing or gloved hand and keep pressure in place.
3. If bleeding is deep and severe, pack the wound firmly if you can do so safely, then continue direct pressure.
4. If there is uncontrolled life-threatening bleeding from an arm or leg, apply a tourniquet above the wound if one is available and you know how to use it.
5. Keep the person lying still, prevent heat loss and watch for signs of shock such as pale skin, fast breathing or confusion.
6. Do not stop monitoring until professional care has taken over.
warning: Do not remove a tourniquet once applied, and do not keep lifting dressings to check the wound if bleeding is still active.
call_ambulance_if: bleeding is heavy, spurting or does not stop with pressure
a tourniquet was needed
the person shows signs of shock
the wound is large, deep or caused by a major crash, machinery or animal bite

title: Suspected Fracture
steps: 1. Ask the person not to move the injured area and support the limb in the position found.
2. Control any bleeding around the injury with gentle direct pressure, avoiding pressure directly over protruding bone.
3. Immobilize the injured area with a splint or sling if available, supporting the joints above and below the fracture.
4. Check fingers or toes beyond the injury for colour, warmth, feeling and movement before and after splinting.
5. Apply a wrapped cold pack if available to help reduce swelling.
6. Keep the person still and arrange medical assessment.
warning: Do not try to force a deformed limb straight unless there is no pulse or the limb is clearly losing blood flow and trained help is not immediately available.
call_ambulance_if: the fracture is open or bone is visible
there is severe deformity, loss of pulse, numbness or increasing pain
the injury involves the hip, pelvis, thigh, multiple bones or follows major trauma
the person cannot be moved safely

title: Burns
steps: 1. Stop the burning process and make the area safe before starting first aid.
2. Remove smouldering clothing and any tight jewellery or watches near the burn unless they are stuck to the skin.
3. Cool the burn with cool running water as soon as possible, without over-cooling the whole person.
4. For chemical burns, remove the chemical if safe and irrigate with large amounts of water.
5. Cover the burn with a clean cloth or sheet and keep the person warm during transport.
6. Arrange prompt medical care.
warning: Do not apply ice, paste, oil, turmeric, toothpaste, raw cotton or other home remedies, and do not break blisters.
call_ambulance_if: the burn is large or deep
the burn affects the face, airway, hands, feet, genitals or major joints
there are electrical, chemical or inhalation burns
the person has trouble breathing or signs of shock

title: Choking
steps: 1. Ask if the person is choking and encourage coughing if they can still cough or speak.
2. If the person cannot cough effectively or make sounds, remove any visible object from the mouth only if it is easy to reach.
3. Give age-appropriate back blows and chest or abdominal thrusts until the object comes out or the person becomes unconscious.
4. If the person becomes unconscious, start CPR and re-check the mouth for visible obstruction between cycles.
5. Keep the person calm and monitor breathing closely even after the object is expelled.
warning: Do not perform blind finger sweeps inside the mouth.
call_ambulance_if: the airway does not clear quickly
the person becomes unconscious
breathing remains noisy, difficult or abnormal after the event
the choking episode happened in a small child, older adult or frail person

title: Suspected Heart Attack
steps: 1. Treat sudden chest pressure, chest pain, breathlessness, cold sweat, nausea, faintness or pain spreading to the arm, jaw or back as an emergency.
2. Call emergency services immediately and have the person stop activity and rest in a comfortable position.
3. Loosen tight clothing, reassure the person and monitor breathing and mental status closely.
4. If the person is conscious, not allergic to aspirin and not actively bleeding, give one 300 mg aspirin to chew while waiting for the ambulance.
5. If the person becomes unresponsive and is not breathing normally, start CPR.
warning: Do not let the person walk around or delay transport to see if the pain passes.
call_ambulance_if: chest pain or pressure lasts more than a few minutes
there is shortness of breath, collapse, confusion or severe weakness
the person has a history of heart disease, diabetes or stroke and new chest symptoms
the person becomes unresponsive

title: Shock
steps: 1. Look for signs of shock such as cold hands or feet, weak fast pulse, capillary refill over 3 seconds, pale skin, fast breathing, confusion or lethargy.
2. Treat the cause immediately if you can: control external bleeding, remove the allergen, or cool severe heat illness while arranging urgent transport.
3. Lay the person flat if possible, unless breathing is easier sitting up or there is an injury that prevents this.
4. Keep the person warm, calm and still, and do not give food or drink if surgery or loss of consciousness is possible.
5. Monitor breathing, mental status and skin colour continuously until advanced help arrives.
warning: Shock is a life-threatening emergency even if the person is still awake and talking.
call_ambulance_if: there are signs of poor perfusion or collapse
shock follows bleeding, trauma, burns, severe infection, allergy or chest pain
the person is getting more confused, drowsy or breathless
you cannot quickly reverse the cause

title: Head Injury
steps: 1. Keep the person still and assume there may also be a neck injury after a crash, fall or blow to the head.
2. Check airway and breathing first and be ready to support the airway while keeping the head and neck aligned.
3. Control scalp bleeding with gentle pressure unless you suspect a depressed skull fracture or there is something embedded.
4. Do not give food or drink, and keep observing for worsening headache, vomiting, confusion, unequal pupils or increasing sleepiness.
5. If vomiting occurs and you must turn the person, roll the whole body together while keeping the neck straight.
warning: Blood or clear fluid from the ears or nose after head trauma can mean a serious skull injury.
call_ambulance_if: the person was unconscious, confused or has memory loss
there is repeated vomiting, seizure, worsening headache or unequal pupils
there is bleeding or fluid from the ears or nose
the injury followed a high-risk crash, fall or assault

title: Suspected Spinal Injury
steps: 1. Suspect spinal injury after major trauma, neck pain, back pain, numbness, weakness or paralysis.
2. Tell the person not to move and manually stabilize the head and neck in the position found.
3. Only move the person if there is immediate danger or if you must move them to open the airway and save life.
4. If you need to turn the person, use a log-roll with the head, neck and body moving together.
5. Keep monitoring breathing, circulation and limb sensation while waiting for advanced help.
warning: Do not twist, sit up or pull on the person unless there is no safer option.
call_ambulance_if: there is neck or back pain after trauma
the person has numbness, tingling, weakness or limb paralysis
the person has altered mental status after an injury
the mechanism was high-risk, such as a road crash, fall from height or diving injury

title: Drowning
steps: 1. Remove the person from the water only if it is safe for you to do so.
2. Open the airway, check breathing immediately and start rescue breathing and CPR if the person is not breathing normally.
3. If the person is breathing, place them where the airway stays clear and monitor closely.
4. Remove wet clothing, dry the person and protect them from hypothermia.
5. Arrange urgent medical assessment even if the person seems to recover, because breathing problems can appear later after immersion.
warning: Do not leave a person alone after a drowning episode, even if they are awake and talking.
call_ambulance_if: the person was unconscious, needed rescue breaths or CPR, or inhaled water
breathing is fast, difficult, noisy or worsening
there is cough, chest discomfort, confusion or extreme tiredness after rescue
the person is a child, older adult or has any other injury

title: Poisoning
steps: 1. Remove the person from the toxic source if this can be done safely, and protect yourself from the exposure.
2. Check airway, breathing and mental status; if the person is very drowsy or unconscious, place them in the recovery position if there is no trauma concern.
3. If the poison is on the skin or clothing, remove contaminated clothing and wash exposed skin with plenty of water.
4. If the poison is in the eyes, rinse continuously with clean water.
5. Keep the product container, plant sample or medication pack to show health professionals or a poison centre.
6. Arrange urgent medical advice and transport.
warning: Do not induce vomiting unless specifically directed by a poison specialist or clinician.
call_ambulance_if: the person has trouble breathing, seizure, confusion or collapse
there was pesticide, chemical, unknown substance or large-dose exposure
the person is a child, pregnant, or has significant symptoms
there are burns in the mouth, drooling or repeated vomiting

title: Eye Injury
steps: 1. If there is chemical exposure, remove contact lenses if easy and irrigate the eye immediately with clean water for a prolonged rinse.
2. If there is an object stuck in the eye or a penetrating injury, do not remove it and do not press on the eye.
3. Ask the person to keep both eyes as still as possible and avoid rubbing.
4. Lightly protect the injured eye without pressure while arranging urgent transport.
5. Keep the head elevated if tolerated and continue monitoring for pain, vision loss or worsening swelling.
warning: Avoid pressure on an injured eye, especially if there is a penetrating object or obvious severe trauma.
call_ambulance_if: there is penetrating eye injury or something embedded
vision is reduced, double or absent
pain is severe or chemical exposure occurred
there was a high-speed injury, explosion or major facial trauma

title: Animal Bite
steps: 1. Move the person away from the animal and make the area safe.
2. For dog, cat, monkey, bat or other mammal bites, irrigate and cleanse the wound thoroughly with soap and running water for about 15 minutes.
3. For suspected venomous snake bite, remove rings or tight items, reassure the person and keep the whole person and the bitten limb still.
4. Cover the wound with a clean dressing and arrange prompt medical care for rabies, tetanus, infection risk or antivenom assessment as needed.
5. If safe, gather information about the animal and the time and location of the bite.
warning: Do not cut, suck, burn or tightly tourniquet a snake bite, and do not use herbal or traditional first-aid methods.
call_ambulance_if: the bite is from a venomous or unknown snake
there is facial bite, deep wound, uncontrolled bleeding or rapid swelling
the person has trouble breathing, weakness or signs of allergic reaction
rabies exposure is possible or the person is a child

title: Heat Stroke
steps: 1. Treat confusion, collapse, very hot skin or loss of consciousness in a hot environment as heat stroke.
2. Move the person to shade or a cool place immediately and remove excess clothing.
3. Start rapid cooling using cool water, wet cloths, fanning or cool bathing if available.
4. If the person is fully awake and not confused, offer sips of water; if confused, vomiting or unconscious, give nothing by mouth.
5. Keep monitoring breathing and mental status while waiting for emergency transport.
warning: Heat stroke is a medical emergency with a high fatality risk; cool first and transport urgently.
call_ambulance_if: there is confusion, collapse, seizure or unconsciousness
the person has very hot skin or stops sweating in severe heat
cooling does not lead to quick improvement
the person is elderly, very young, pregnant or has chronic disease

title: Hypothermia
steps: 1. Move the person indoors or to shelter as soon as it is safe.
2. Remove wet clothing, dry the skin and wrap the person in dry blankets or towels, covering the head as well.
3. Give a warm sweet drink only if the person is fully awake and able to swallow safely.
4. Keep the person still, talk to them to keep them awake and stay with them until help arrives.
5. Monitor breathing closely and be ready to start CPR if normal breathing stops.
warning: Do not use very hot water, direct heaters or vigorous rubbing to warm the person.
call_ambulance_if: the person is confused, unusually sleepy or hard to wake
shivering stops or breathing becomes slow
there are signs of frostbite
the person is an infant, older adult or has other injuries

title: Electric Shock
steps: 1. Do not touch the person until the power source is switched off or the person is safely separated from the current.
2. Once safe, check responsiveness and breathing immediately.
3. Start CPR if the person is unresponsive and not breathing normally.
4. Look for burns or trauma from a fall, cool obvious burns with cool water and cover them with a clean cloth.
5. Keep the person still and arrange urgent medical evaluation because internal injuries or heart rhythm problems may not be obvious.
warning: Your safety comes first; never become a second casualty by grabbing a person who is still in contact with electricity.
call_ambulance_if: the shock was from high voltage or lightning
the person lost consciousness, has chest pain or abnormal breathing
there are burns, muscle pain, weakness or fall injuries
the person is pregnant or a child

title: Severe Allergic Reaction
steps: 1. Suspect a severe allergic reaction if there is facial swelling, wheeze, stridor, rash with breathing trouble, collapse or shock after food, medicine, bite or sting exposure.
2. Remove the trigger if this can be done safely, and call emergency services immediately.
3. If an adrenaline auto-injector or other IM adrenaline is available, give it promptly according to the device or local instructions.
4. Keep the person calm and in the position that helps breathing best; if they faint, lay them flat unless breathing is worse that way.
5. Monitor closely because symptoms can recur and further doses may be needed while waiting for advanced care.
warning: Do not wait for a rash to appear before treating breathing difficulty or collapse as anaphylaxis.
call_ambulance_if: there is any breathing difficulty, facial swelling or collapse
adrenaline was used
symptoms are returning or worsening
the reaction followed a bite, sting, medicine or food exposure

title: Diabetic Emergency
steps: 1. Suspect a diabetic emergency if a person with diabetes has confusion, unusual behaviour, sweating, weakness, deep breathing, vomiting, dehydration or reduced consciousness.
2. Check blood glucose if a meter is available; if you cannot check and low blood sugar is possible, treat promptly.
3. If the person is awake and can swallow safely, give oral glucose, a sugary drink or other fast sugar.
4. If the person is drowsy, vomiting or unconscious, give nothing by mouth and place them in the recovery position if there is no trauma concern.
5. Monitor mental status and breathing closely and arrange urgent medical care, especially if high blood sugar or ketoacidosis is suspected.
warning: Do not force food or drink into the mouth of anyone with reduced consciousness.
call_ambulance_if: the person is unconscious, has a seizure or is not improving quickly after sugar
there is deep or rapid breathing, repeated vomiting or severe dehydration
blood glucose is very high or very low
you are unsure whether the problem is low sugar or diabetic ketoacidosis

title: Seizure
steps: 1. Protect the person from injury by clearing nearby hazards and cushioning the head.
2. Do not hold the person down and do not put anything in their mouth.
3. Time the seizure and watch for breathing problems or repeated convulsions.
4. After the convulsion stops, place the person in the recovery position if there is no trauma concern and keep the airway clear.
5. Stay with the person until they are fully awake and continue to monitor because prolonged altered mental status needs urgent assessment.
warning: Repeated seizures or prolonged confusion after a seizure require urgent medical evaluation.
call_ambulance_if: the seizure lasts more than 5 minutes
multiple seizures occur without full recovery between them
the person was injured, pregnant, in water, or this is a first seizure
breathing is difficult or consciousness does not improve

title: Suspected Stroke
steps: 1. Use the stroke warning signs: sudden balance loss, sudden vision change, facial droop, arm weakness and speech difficulty.
2. Treat suspected stroke as a medical emergency and call emergency services immediately.
3. Note the exact time the person was last known well, because rapid treatment is critical.
4. Keep the person safe and resting, and do not give food, drink or oral medicines.
5. Monitor breathing and mental status closely and be ready to start CPR if the person stops breathing normally.
warning: Do not wait to see if symptoms go away; rapid transfer can determine whether time-sensitive stroke treatment is possible.
call_ambulance_if: there is sudden facial droop, arm weakness or speech difficulty
there is sudden severe headache, collapse, confusion or one-sided numbness
vision suddenly changes or balance is lost without explanation
the person becomes drowsy or unresponsive
